structured_choices.data_source: ICU and general ward EHR records from a multi-hospital health system spanning 3–4 years. Inclusion: all admissions ≥18 years with ≥6 hours of observation time post-admission and complete vitals, lab, medication, and note records. Exclusion: transfers between hospitals (confounds note availability), patients with sepsis diagnosis at or before admission (prevents 6-hour prediction window), and records with >20% missing vital signs in any 1-hour window.
structured_choices.label_definition: Sepsis onset is defined using a consensus chart-review protocol applied by trained clinicians to a stratified sample (all suspected sepsis cases plus 10% random negatives) based on SEPSIS-3 criteria (suspected infection + organ dysfunction), with the event timestamp set to the earliest documented time sepsis criteria were first met. For all admissions, binary labels are: onset within next 6 hours (positive) or no sepsis within 72 hours of the prediction window start (negative). Admissions with sepsis onset >6 hours from prediction window or with incomplete follow-up are excluded.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split by admission date, stratified by hospital site and baseline sepsis risk (based on age, admission type, and Elixhauser comorbidity index quartiles). Training set: first 60% of admissions chronologically; validation set: next 20%; test set: final 20%. This temporal ordering prevents leakage from future documentation or post-onset treatment artifacts, and site/risk stratification ensures representative distribution across the deployment target (multi-hospital system with varying case mix). Stratification within each split maintains sepsis class balance at ~3–5% positive rate across all three sets.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit exclusively on the training set. Scaling parameters (mean, std for vitals/labs), categorical encoders (medication/diagnosis vocabularies), note tokenizers, and transformer embeddings are derived from training data only. Validation and test sets are transformed using these training-fit parameters without retraining or adjustment. This prevents information leakage and ensures the model encounters realistic distribution shift at validation/test time.
structured_choices.features: Structured features: (1) Vital signs time series: rolling 1-hour windows of heart rate, blood pressure, temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, with mean, std, min, max, and trend (linear slope) computed over the preceding 6 hours; (2) Lab values: most recent result and trend (delta from 24 hours prior) for CBC, CMP, lactate, procalcitonin, where available; (3) Medication features: binary indicators for vasopressors, antibiotics, and immunosuppressants in the preceding 24 hours; (4) Demographics and comorbidity: age, admission type, Elixhauser index. Unstructured features: (1) Nursing note embeddings: all notes timestamped in the 6-hour window before prediction are concatenated in chronological order, truncated to pre-prediction timestamps only (no post-event documentation), tokenized, and passed through a pre-trained clinical BERT model (e.g., SciBERT or clinical DistilBERT) to produce sequence embeddings; attention pooling reduces the sequence to a fixed-length vector. No free-text preprocessing (lemmatization, stop-word removal) is applied; the transformer handles tokenization. Notes are excluded if their timestamp post-dates the prediction window (stricter control for note-timing confound).
structured_choices.model: Two competing models are trained in parallel: (1) Structured-only gradient-boosted model (XGBoost or LightGBM) using demographic, vital, lab, and medication features only, tuned via Bayesian optimization over learning rate, tree depth, and regularization. (2) Hybrid model: structured features are concatenated with nursing note embeddings (from clinical BERT), and the concatenated feature vector is passed to an XGBoost model with equivalent tuning budget and search space. Both models output predicted probability of sepsis onset within 6 hours.
structured_choices.baseline: The structured-only gradient-boosted model serves as the baseline. It receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort (same Bayesian optimization budget: 50 iterations, same cross-validation folds on training data), the same feature engineering rigor (rolling statistics, lag features), and identical evaluation on the held-out test set under the same metrics. The baseline is chosen because it represents clinical practice (structured EHR features are always available and well-validated) and isolates the contribution of free-text embeddings. A naive logistic regression on raw counts of clinical events would be weaker but not a fair comparison; both models use boosting to control for nonlinearity.
structured_choices.primary_metric: AUROC (area under the receiver operating characteristic curve) on the held-out test set. Justification: clinicians operate under variable alert thresholds, and AUROC captures the model's ability to rank high-risk patients correctly across all possible thresholds. This aligns with the clinical goal of reducing missed sepsis cases at acceptable false-alert rates. AUROC is also insensitive to the class imbalance (~3–5% positive rate) and does not require threshold selection a priori.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity and specificity at the threshold that maximizes Youden's index on the validation set (clinically interpretable operating point)
Precision-recall curve and PR-AUC (particularly informative under class imbalance; captures trade-off between catching cases and minimizing false alerts)

Calibration: Brier score and calibration plots (binned predicted probability vs. observed frequency) to assess whether predicted probabilities reflect true risk and are actionable for clinical decision-making
Stratified performance: AUROC within each hospital site and each baseline risk quartile, to detect site-level or population-specific failures
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed via nested cross-validation on the training set only: inner loop (5-fold stratified cross-validation within training data) for Bayesian optimization; outer loop (holdout validation set) for final model selection. The model configuration (hyperparameters, feature set, architecture choices) that maximizes AUROC on the validation set is locked in before any evaluation on the test set. The test set is not inspected until final reporting.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held out and untouched during model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and any exploratory analysis. After both models are fully trained and locked (including all hyperparameter choices), the test set is used exactly once to compute the primary and secondary metrics. No further tuning or threshold adjustment is performed after test-set evaluation. All reported performance figures are from the single test-set evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Three confounds are explicitly addressed: (1) Note timing and leakage: notes are truncated to pre-prediction timestamps only, and any note dated after the prediction window is excluded entirely. A robustness check repeats the analysis with notes truncated to only the first 3 hours of the 6-hour window to test whether the gain is driven by late (post-suspicion) notes. (2) Documentation intensity: both models' feature sets include a 'note count in preceding 6 hours' variable, and stratified analysis compares performance within quintiles of note intensity to detect whether the hybrid model gains are driven purely by observation intensity rather than semantic content. (3) Patient acuity confounding: both the split stratification and secondary metrics stratify by baseline sepsis risk (Elixhauser quartiles and admission type); if the hybrid model helps only in high-acuity patients (more detailed notes), that is reported separately as a population-specific finding rather than a universal improvement. The experiment tests whether the hybrid model achieves higher AUROC; it does not and cannot establish that the free-text signal is causally necessary for improved prediction—only that it provides empirical gain under realistic EHR conditions.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Structured + Free-Text Embeddings for Sepsis Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort Definition

We source 3–4 years of EHR records from a multi-hospital system, including all inpatient admissions ≥18 years old with ≥6 hours of observation and complete records of vitals, labs, medications, and clinical notes. We exclude transfers between hospitals (which confound note availability), patients with sepsis at admission (which collapse the prediction window), and admissions with >20% missing vitals in any 1-hour block. All admissions are assigned binary labels via consensus chart review: sepsis onset within the next 6 hours (positive) or no sepsis within 72 hours (negative). Admissions with sepsis onset 6–72 hours after the prediction window or incomplete follow-up are excluded. This produces a cohort of tens of thousands of admissions with ~3–5% positive rate, reflecting clinical prevalence.

### 2. Split Strategy and Temporal Ordering

We divide the cohort chronologically: first 60% of admissions by date go to training, next 20% to validation, final 20% to test. Within each split, we stratify by hospital site and baseline sepsis risk quartile (derived from age, admission type, and Elixhauser score) to ensure representative case mix across all three sets. This temporal ordering is critical: it prevents leakage from future documentation (post-onset clinical notes often discuss sepsis suspicion or treatment, encoding the label) and from treatment-induced vital changes. Temporal splits reflect real deployment: the model will encounter future data with potentially different documentation patterns, and a chronological evaluation tests this realistic shift. Stratification ensures both the structured and free-text models face identical class and risk distributions, making the comparison fair.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing is fit on training data only and applied (never refitted) to validation and test.

**Structured features:** For each patient at the prediction window, we compute: (a) rolling 1-hour vital statistics (heart rate, BP, temp, RR, SpO₂) over the preceding 6 hours: mean, std, min, max, and linear trend; (b) most recent lab values (CBC, CMP, lactate, procalcitonin) and their 24-hour delta; (c) binary flags for vasopressors, antibiotics, immunosuppressants in the prior 24 hours; (d) demographics (age, admission type, Elixhauser index). These features are scaled to zero mean and unit variance (fit on training only).

**Unstructured features:** All nursing notes timestamped in the 6-hour prediction window are identified and **truncated to timestamps strictly before the prediction time** (strong control for note-timing leakage). Notes are concatenated in chronological order and passed through a pre-trained clinical BERT model (SciBERT or clinical DistilBERT) to produce contextualized token embeddings. Attention pooling aggregates the token-level embeddings into a single fixed-length vector per patient. No manual preprocessing (stop-word removal, lemmatization) is applied; the transformer handles it. A secondary feature—count of notes in the 6-hour window—is included in both models to control for documentation intensity.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Baseline (Structured-Only):** Gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on structured features alone, using Bayesian optimization for 50 iterations to tune learning rate, max depth, and L2 regularization. The model is selected based on maximum AUROC in 5-fold cross-validation on the training set.

**Proposed (Hybrid):** Concatenate structured features with nursing note embeddings (from clinical BERT), then train an XGBoost model on the combined feature set. Hyperparameter tuning uses the same Bayesian optimization budget (50 iterations), same cross-validation strategy, and same feature engineering rigor as the baseline. This ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of free-text embeddings, not unequal tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** AUROC on the held-out test set. We choose AUROC because clinicians operate at variable alert thresholds; AUROC captures discrimination ability across all thresholds and is robust to class imbalance. The hypothesis is confirmed if the hybrid model achieves higher test-set AUROC than the baseline.

**Secondary metrics:** (1) Sensitivity and specificity at the Youden-optimal threshold on the validation set (interpretable operating point); (2) Precision-recall curve and PR-AUC (informative under imbalance); (3) Brier score and calibration plots (ensures predicted probabilities are actionable for clinicians); (4) Stratified AUROC within each hospital site and sepsis-risk quartile (detects population-specific failures or gains).

### 6. Model Selection and Test-Set Policy

Both models are tuned via nested cross-validation on the training set: inner loop performs Bayesian optimization over 5 stratified folds; outer loop (validation set) selects the final configuration. Once both models are locked (all hyperparameters fixed), the test set is used exactly once to compute all metrics. No threshold adjustment, re-tuning, or exploratory analysis occurs after test-set evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

**Note-timing leakage:** Notes are truncated to pre-prediction timestamps. A robustness check repeats the analysis with notes restricted to only the first 3 hours of the window; if the hybrid gain persists, it is more robust to late-note confounding.

**Documentation intensity:** Both models include note count as a feature, and stratified analysis compares performance across note-intensity quintiles. If the hybrid model helps only when documentation is heavy, this is reported as a population-specific finding.

**Acuity:** Stratified metrics within baseline risk quartiles and admission types distinguish universal gains from acuity-driven gains.

**Scope:** The experiment tests whether the hybrid model achieves empirical gain under realistic EHR conditions. It does not establish that free-text embeddings are causally necessary; causality requires intervention or counterfactual reasoning beyond this observational design.
